  7. For 33 residues on CPU: ~1-2 minutes

  8. For 33 residues on GPU: ~5 seconds

  9. Explore the 3D structure:

  10. Drag the protein to rotate
  11. Scroll to zoom

  12. Right-click drag to pan

  13. Switch to confidence coloring:

  14. Click pLDDT colors at the bottom-left
  15. Blue regions are high confidence

  16. Orange/red regions are low confidence (often loops or disordered)

  17. Save the structure:

  18. Click Save PDB in the header
  19. The PDB file downloads to your computer
  20. You can open it in any other molecular viewer (PyMOL, ChimeraX, etc.)

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• How to fold proteins with ESMFold
  • 3D viewer controls
  • Confidence visualization
  • Exporting PDB files

docs.title: Tutorial 3: Design Alternative Sequences with ESM-IF1 (15 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Take a folded protein and generate alternative sequences that should fold into the same shape.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. First, fold a protein (use Tutorial 2's sequence or any other)

  2. Click "Design 10 Sequences (ESM-IF1)"

  3. First time: ESM-IF1 model downloads (~600 MB)
  4. Generates 10 alternative sequences

  5. Each is scored by recovery (% of residues matching the original)

  6. Review the results:

  7. A panel appears with the 10 candidates ranked by score
  8. Notice the recovery percentages (typically 4-30% with default temperature)
  9. High recovery = conservative redesign

  10. Low recovery = aggressive redesign of the same fold

  11. Verify a candidate by folding it:

  12. Click Fold on candidate #1
  13. This folds the new sequence

  14. Add it to your structures list

  15. Compare original vs designed:

  16. Switch to the Structures tab
  17. Click vs on the original structure
  18. The viewer splits to show both side-by-side
  19. Visually compare — they should have similar topology if ESM-IF1 worked

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• How inverse folding works
  • Sequence recovery interpretation
  • How to verify designed sequences by re-folding
  • Side-by-side structure comparison

docs.title: Tutorial 4: Iterative Design Loop (30 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Use OpenDNA's killer feature — automated protein optimization over multiple rounds.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Pick a starting sequence: 

    MKTVRQERLKSIVRILER
    (18 residues — small enough to iterate quickly on CPU)

  2. Configure iterative design:

  3. In the Iterative Design Loop section
  4. Set rounds: 3 and candidates: 5

  5. That's 15 total designs across 3 rounds

  6. Click "Run Iterative Design"

  7. You'll see a series of toasts as each round runs
  8. Rounds typically take 1-3 minutes each on CPU

  9. Total: ~10 minutes

  10. Review the results panel:

  11. Initial score → Final score with improvement %
  12. Optimization history chart
  13. Best sequence per round

  14. The final structure is auto-added to your structures list

  15. Compare initial and final:

  16. Initial sequence is in your history
  17. Final sequence is the new active structure
  18. Use the vs button to compare them in 3D

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• The iterative optimization concept
  • How to read the score history
  • How to interpret improvement
  • The fold ↔ design feedback loop

docs.title: Try this variation
docs.text: Set rounds to 5, candidates to 10. Watch a longer optimization. See if it converges or keeps improving.

docs.title: Tutorial 5: Famous Mutations to Try
docs.text: These are real mutations from biology and disease. Try them in OpenDNA.

docs.title: Tumor suppressors and oncogenes
docs.text:
KRAS G12D (most common cancer mutation): 1. Import → kras 2. Mutate → G12D 3. This is the famous G12D KRAS mutation found in pancreatic cancer

p53 R175H (tumor suppressor mutation): 1. Import → p53 2. Mutate → R175H 3. This is one of the most common p53 mutations in human cancer

docs.title: Insulin variants
docs.text: Insulin lispro (Humalog) — switch the position of B28 and B29 to make a fast-acting insulin - Import insulin → mutate K28P then P29K (sequentially)

docs.title: Hemoglobin S (sickle cell)
docs.text:
• Import hemoglobin_alpha → not the right chain
  • Try fetching P68871 (hemoglobin beta) → mutate E6V

docs.title: CFTR ΔF508 (cystic fibrosis)
docs.text:
• Fetch P13569 (CFTR)
  • Note: very large protein, don't fold on CPU

docs.title: Tutorial 6: Use the Command Palette (3 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Master the keyboard-driven workflow.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open the palette anywhere: Press Ctrl+K (or Cmd+K on Mac)

  2. Try fuzzy search:

  3. Type "ubiq" → finds "Import ubiquitin"
  4. Type "fold" → finds the fold action

  5. Type "score" → finds the score action

  6. Quick imports:

  7. Ctrl+K → "import gfp" → Enter

  8. The sequence loads automatically

  9. Quick analysis:

  10. Ctrl+K → "analyze" → Enter

  11. Skips clicking

  12. Navigate without a mouse:

  13. Ctrl+K → up/down arrows → Enter
  14. Esc to close

docs.title: Power user tip
docs.text: With single-letter shortcuts (only when not focused on an input): - F = fold current sequence - S = score - A = analyze - Esc = close everything

docs.title: Tutorial 7: The Protein Academy (15 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Learn protein basics through interactive games.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Click Academy in the header

  2. Level 1: Amino Acid Match (5 min, +50 XP):

  3. Click letters and names to match them up
  4. 8 random amino acids per game
  5. +10 score per match

  6. Complete to earn 50 XP

  7. Level 2: Memory Quiz (3 min, +75 XP):

  8. 5 multiple-choice questions about amino acid properties

  9. Test your knowledge of charge, structure, function

  10. Level 3: Sequence Reader (5 min, +100 XP):

  11. Tutorial on reading protein sequences
  12. Learn the meaning of each letter

  13. Pattern recognition tips

  14. Watch your XP grow in the header badge

docs.title: Educational notes
docs.text:
• The 20 standard amino acids are the building blocks of all proteins
  • Their letter codes (A, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, S, T, V, W, Y) are universally used
  • Each has different properties: charge, size, hydrophobicity, special function
  • Reading a sequence and predicting properties is the foundational skill

docs.title: Tutorial 8: Compare Two Structures (10 minutes)
docs.text: Goal: Use OpenDNA to compare a wild-type protein and a mutant.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Fold the wild-type:
  2. Paste: MKTVRQERLKSIVRILER
  3. Click Predict Structure

  4. Wait for the fold

  5. Apply a mutation:

  6. Mutate field: K2D
  7. Click Apply Mutation & Refold

  8. Wait for the new fold

  9. Compare them:

  10. Click the Structures tab
  11. You should see 2 structures
  12. Click vs on the first one (the wild-type)

  13. The viewer splits into two

  14. Visually inspect:

  15. Are they similar topology?
  16. Did the mutation cause any visible changes?

  17. Use both viewers' rotation independently

  18. Quantitative comparison (via API): 

    curl -X POST http://localhost:8765/v1/compare \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"pdb_a": "...", "pdb_b": "..."}'
    Returns RMSD, secondary structure identity, radius of gyration.

docs.title: Tutorial 9: Estimate the Cost of Your Protein
docs.text: Goal: Find out how much it would cost to actually synthesize and produce your designed protein.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Have a sequence loaded (any size)

  2. Click Cost & Carbon Estimate

  3. Review:

  4. Synthesis costs from Twist Bioscience, IDT, GenScript
  5. Cheapest vendor highlighted

  6. Carbon footprint comparison: CPU vs GPU compute

  7. Use this to plan budget for wet-lab experiments

docs.title: What the estimates mean
docs.text:
• Synthesis = ordering the DNA gene that codes for your protein
  • After ordering: clone into vector → transform into E. coli → grow → purify → assay
  • Total wet-lab cost typically 5-20x the synthesis cost

docs.title: Tutorial 10: Save and Reload a Project
docs.text: Goal: Don't lose your work between sessions.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Build up some structures (fold a few proteins)

  2. Save:

  3. Press Ctrl+Shift+S (or use Cmd+K → "save project")
  4. Enter a project name (e.g. my_first_project)

  5. Toast confirms save

  6. Find your saved project:

  7. Open the Dashboard (header button)

  8. "Saved Projects" section lists all your projects

  9. Load a project (manually via API for now): 

    curl -X POST http://localhost:8765/v1/projects/load \
  -d '{"name":"my_first_project"}'

  10. UI auto-load is coming in v0.3

docs.title: Where projects live
docs.text: ~/.opendna/projects/<name>/workspace.json

docs.title: Tutorial 11: Install and use DiffDock via the Component Manager
docs.text: Goal: Install the DiffDock docking engine from inside the desktop app and dock a small-molecule ligand against a folded protein.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Desktop app or browser UI running
  • ~1.5 GB free disk space
  • A folded structure (we'll use ubiquitin from the Import tab)

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open the Component Manager
  2. Click Components in the header

  3. You'll see 11 engine cards. Filter to the Docking category.

  4. Install DiffDock

  5. Click Install on the DiffDock card
  6. A progress bar streams from POST /v1/components/install
  7. Wait ~2 minutes for the weights to download (~1.1 GB)

  8. Status changes to Installed when done

  9. Load your receptor

  10. Close the Component Manager
  11. Import tab → click ubiquitin

  12. Click Predict Structure (ESMFold must be installed — if not, install it first)

  13. Supply a ligand

  14. In the sidebar, open the Docking section
  15. Paste a SMILES string for your ligand: 
    CC(=O)OC1=CC=CC=C1C(=O)O

  16. (That's aspirin — a nice small test ligand.)

  17. Run the dock via CLI equivalent 

    curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8765/v1/dock \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "receptor_pdb": "...",
    "ligand_smiles": "CC(=O)OC1=CC=CC=C1C(=O)O",
    "engine": "diffdock",
    "num_poses": 10
  }'

  18. View the results

  19. The viewer displays the top-scoring pose by default
  20. Use the pose slider at the bottom to flip through ranked poses
  21. Each pose carries an affinity estimate

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• How the Component Manager gates heavy ML engines
  • How DiffDock differs from AutoDock Vina (diffusion vs. lattice search)
  • How to inspect pose rankings from the UI and the API

docs.title: Next
docs.text:
• Try AutoDock Vina via "engine": "vina" and compare
  • Feed the best pose into an MD run to see if it stays bound

docs.title: Tutorial 12: Build a fold → design → evaluate workflow in the Visual Editor
docs.text: Goal: Use the visual workflow editor to build a reproducible pipeline without writing any Python.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open the editor
  2. Click Workflow in the header

  3. The canvas opens empty

  4. Load a template

  5. Click Templates → Fold → Design → Evaluate

  6. A pre-built 5-node graph appears: 

    [Sequence] → [Fold] → [Design] → [Fold] → [Score]

  7. Configure the Sequence node

  8. Double-click the Sequence node

  9. Paste ubiquitin: 

    MQIFVKTLTGKTITLEVEPSDTIENVKAKIQDKEGIPPDQQRLIFAGKQLEDGRTLSDYNIQKESTLHLVLRLRGG

  10. Configure the Design node

  11. Double-click the Design node
  12. Set engine: esm_if1

  13. Candidates: 10

  14. Run

  15. Click Run in the toolbar
  16. Nodes light up green as they complete. Progress is streamed via WebSocket.

  17. Total runtime: ~4 minutes on GPU, ~25 minutes on CPU

  18. Save the workflow

  19. Click Save → YAML
  20. Name it ubiquitin-redesign.yaml

  21. It lands in ~/.opendna/workflows/

  22. Inspect the provenance

  23. Click History in the bottom bar
  24. You'll see one DAG node per workflow step with inputs, outputs, and hashes

  25. Drag the timeline handle back to the Design step to inspect the 10 candidates

  26. Re-run from YAML via CLI 

    opendna workflow run ~/.opendna/workflows/ubiquitin-redesign.yaml

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• How to use templates as a starting point
  • How node types and edges enforce type safety
  • How provenance is automatically recorded
  • How to save / replay workflows without opening the UI

docs.title: Tutorial 13: Real-time collaboration with a teammate via Yjs
docs.text: Goal: Co-edit a project with a colleague in real time.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Start a room
  2. Open your project in OpenDNA
  3. Click Collab in the header
  4. Click New room

  5. Copy the room URL (it looks like opendna://collab/8a4f...)

  6. Share it

  7. Send the URL to your teammate via Slack / Teams / email

  8. They click it, the app opens, and they join the same project

  9. Use the Notes tab

  10. Both of you can type in the shared Markdown document
  11. You'll see each other's cursors live

  12. Try **bold**, # headings, ```python code blocks

  13. Pin residue comments

  14. Click residue K48 in the viewer
  15. Click Comment
  16. Type "Suspected catalytic nucleophile — let's mutate to R"

  17. Your teammate sees the comment instantly and can reply

  18. Simulate offline tolerance

  19. Disable your Wi-Fi
  20. Keep editing the notes

  21. Re-enable Wi-Fi — the Yjs CRDT auto-merges without conflicts

  22. Verify sync via the API 

    curl http://127.0.0.1:8765/v1/collab/rooms/<room-id>
    Shows current participants and last-update timestamp.

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• How Yjs CRDTs give conflict-free real-time editing
  • How comments are pinned to residues and persist forever
  • How the system tolerates network interruptions

docs.title: Tutorial 14: Search UniProt for cancer proteins and fold the top hit
docs.text: Goal: Use the unified search to find a relevant protein and fold it.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open the search overlay

  2. Click Search in the header (or press Ctrl+Shift+F)

  3. Search with filters

  4. Query: cancer kinase
  5. Check Reviewed only
  6. Organism: Homo sapiens
  7. Length: 100–500

  8. Click Search

  9. Inspect results

  10. You'll see ~20 Swiss-Prot reviewed hits
  11. Each row: accession, name, length, review badge

  12. Click the first result — say, BRAF_HUMAN

  13. Fold it

  14. Back in the Tools tab the sequence is loaded
  15. Click Predict Structure

  16. ESMFold runs (installed from Tutorial 11 or Component Manager)

  17. Analyze

  18. Click Full Analysis Suite

  19. Look at disorder (BRAF has a disordered N-terminus), binding pockets (the ATP pocket should rank #1)

  20. Equivalent cURL 

    curl "http://127.0.0.1:8765/v1/search/uniprot?q=cancer+kinase&reviewed=true&organism=Homo+sapiens"

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• How to compose filters in the unified search
  • How results integrate seamlessly with the Tools tab
  • How to reach the same functionality from the API

docs.title: Tutorial 15: Enable PQC auth and create API keys
docs.text: Goal: Turn on post-quantum authentication and mint a scoped API key.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
pip install "opendna[pqc]"
# macOS
brew install liboqs
# Debian/Ubuntu
sudo apt install liboqs-dev

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Start the server with auth required 

    export OPENDNA_AUTH_REQUIRED=1
export OPENDNA_AUTH_DB=~/.opendna/auth.db
python -c "from opendna.api.server import start_server; start_server(port=8765)"

  2. Register the first user (admin) 

    curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8765/v1/auth/register \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"username": "alice", "password": "correct horse battery staple"}'

Response: 

{
  "user_id": "u_001",
  "role": "admin",
  "api_key": "odna_pqc_9f3a...",
  "ml_dsa_public_key": "...base64..."
}

Save the API key now — it is only shown once.

  1. Test it 

    export KEY="odna_pqc_9f3a..."
curl http://127.0.0.1:8765/v1/auth/me -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY"

  2. Create a scoped API key for a CI pipeline 

    curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8765/v1/auth/api-keys \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "name": "github-ci",
    "scopes": ["fold", "analyze"],
    "expires_in_days": 90
  }'

  3. Register a second user 

    curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8765/v1/auth/register \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"username": "bob", "password": "..."}'
    Bob is created as a regular user (not admin) because he is registered by alice.

  4. Verify the audit log 

    opendna audit verify
opendna audit tail --n 20

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• How to enable PQC auth
  • How the first-user-is-admin pattern works
  • How to mint scoped API keys for automation
  • How the hash-chained audit log is verified end-to-end

docs.title: Tutorial 16: Mint a Zenodo DOI for your project
docs.text: Goal: Publish a reproducible package of your project to Zenodo and get a citable DOI.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• A Zenodo account (free at zenodo.org)
  • A personal access token from zenodo.org/account/settings/applications/
  • An OpenDNA project with at least one folded structure

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Set your token 

    export ZENODO_TOKEN="your_token_here"

  2. Open the Lab Notebook

  3. In the desktop app, click Save → Notebook

  4. Fill in at least:

    • Title (e.g. "Computational redesign of ubiquitin K48")
    • Authors (e.g. "Alice Smith; Bob Jones")
    • Description / abstract
    • Keywords (comma-separated)

  5. Click Publish → Zenodo

  6. OpenDNA bundles:
    • Provenance DAG (YAML)
    • Every structure (PDB)
    • Every figure (PNG + SVG)
    • Notebook (Markdown + PDF)
    • Workflow YAML (if present)
    • CycloneDX SBOM
    • Manifest with SHA-256 hashes

  7. Uploaded as a single .zip

  8. Confirm

  9. The response dialog shows the DOI: 
    10.5281/zenodo.12345678

  10. Click Copy DOI or Open on Zenodo

  11. CLI alternative 

    opendna publish zenodo \
  --project ~/.opendna/projects/ubiquitin_study \
  --title "Computational redesign of ubiquitin K48" \
  --author "Alice Smith" \
  --keyword "protein,design,ESMFold"

  12. Re-fetch your published package later 

    opendna fetch zenodo 10.5281/zenodo.12345678
    This pulls the .zip, verifies hashes, and reconstructs the project locally — a complete reproducibility round-trip.

docs.title: What you learned
docs.text:
• How the lab notebook bundles a project into a single artifact
  • How to mint a DOI programmatically
  • How to round-trip a published project for reproducibility

docs.title: Tutorial Cheat Sheet
docs.text: Goal Click path Fold a protein Tools → paste seq → Predict Structure Score instantly Tools → paste seq → Score Protein Full analysis Tools → Full Analysis Suite Apply mutation Tools → Mutate field → Apply Design alternatives Tools → Design 10 Sequences Iterative optimization Tools → Run Iterative Design Import famous protein Import → click name (or Cmd+K → "import X") Compare two structures Structures tab → vs button AI explanation Tools → Explain This Protein Cost estimate Tools → Cost & Carbon Estimate Quick MD Tools → Quick MD Open Dashboard Header → Dashboard Open Academy Header → Academy Command palette Ctrl+K Save project Ctrl+Shift+S Save PDB Ctrl+S or header button

docs.title: OpenDNA User Guide
docs.text: The complete reference for using OpenDNA. Every feature, every button, every panel.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Interface Overview
  2. The Sidebar
  3. The 3D Viewer
  4. The Chat Panel
  5. The Header Bar
  6. The Command Palette
  7. Notifications (Toasts)
  8. Analysis Panel
  9. Dashboard
  10. Protein Academy
  11. Iterative Design
  12. Keyboard Shortcuts

docs.title: The Sidebar
docs.text: The sidebar has 3 tabs:

docs.title: Tools Tab
docs.text: The main workspace. Contains:

docs.title: Protein Sequence
docs.text:
• Textarea — Paste any amino acid sequence here. Updates in real-time as you import or edit.
  • Length indicator — Shows residue count once a sequence is loaded.
  • Predict Structure button — Submits to ESMFold (folds the protein in 3D).
  • Score Protein button — Instant sequence-only quality score.

docs.title: Iterative Design Loop
docs.text:
• Rounds × Candidates controls — Set how many optimization rounds and how many candidates per round.
  • Run Iterative Design button — Automated optimization loop.

docs.title: Mutate Active Protein
docs.text:
• Mutation input — Format like K48R (from-position-to). 1-indexed.
  • Apply Mutation & Refold — Applies the mutation and immediately refolds the new sequence.

docs.title: Active Structure Tools
docs.text: Available actions on the currently-loaded protein: - Design 10 Sequences (ESM-IF1) — Generate 10 alternative sequences for the active backbone. - Full Analysis Suite — Open the comprehensive analysis panel. - Explain This Protein (AI) — Get a plain-English explanation (uses Ollama if available). - Quick MD (stability check) — Run a fast molecular dynamics check. - Cost & Carbon Estimate — Show synthesis $$ and CO₂ estimates.

docs.title: Structures Tab
docs.text: History of all folded structures in the current session. - Click any structure to make it active in the viewer. - Click the vs button to compare it side-by-side with the active structure. - Active structure has a highlighted border. - Each shows: name preview, length, mean pLDDT confidence.

docs.title: Import Tab
docs.text: Load proteins from external databases. - Source toggle — UniProt or PDB - ID input — Enter accession (e.g. P0CG48) or famous name (e.g. ubiquitin) - Fetch button — Loads the protein and switches to the Tools tab - Famous proteins grid — Quick-access for ubiquitin, insulin, GFP, lysozyme, myoglobin, p53, KRAS, EGFR

docs.title: The 3D Viewer
docs.text: The main visualization area, powered by Molstar.

docs.title: Mouse Controls
docs.text:
• Drag — Rotate
  • Scroll — Zoom
  • Right-click drag — Translate
  • Click an atom — Show info popup

docs.title: Color Modes (bottom-left buttons)
docs.text:
• pLDDT colors — AlphaFold-style confidence coloring (blue=high, red=low)
  • Chain colors — Color by chain ID (default Molstar)

docs.title: Stats Overlay (top-right)
docs.text: Shows total atoms and residues in the loaded structure.

docs.title: Side-by-Side Compare
docs.text: When you click "vs" on a structure in the Structures tab, the viewer splits in half showing both structures simultaneously. Each viewer can be controlled independently.

docs.title: The Chat Panel
docs.text: A natural-language interface at the bottom of the main area.

docs.title: What you can say
docs.text:
• fold MKTVRQERLKSIVRILER — Predicts structure for any sequence
  • score MKTVRQERLK — Evaluates a sequence
  • mutate K48R — Applies a mutation to the active protein
  • explain — Gets an AI explanation of the active protein
  • help — Lists available commands

docs.title: Behind the scenes
docs.text:
The chat panel sends your message to /v1/chat which: 1. Tries Ollama (llama3.2:3b) if running 2. Falls back to a deterministic regex-based intent parser 3. Maps the intent to UI actions

If you install Ollama and run ollama pull llama3.2:3b, you get richer natural language understanding for free.

docs.title: Left side
docs.text:
• Logo — OpenDNA brand
  • Version — Current build version (e.g. v0.2.0-beta)
  • XP badge — Your accumulated experience points from Academy

docs.title: Right side
docs.text:
• ⌘K button — Opens the command palette (or press Ctrl+K)
  • Dashboard button — Opens the Dashboard overlay
  • Academy button — Opens the Protein Academy
  • Save PDB — Downloads the active structure (only shown when one is loaded)
  • ☀/☾ — Toggle dark/light theme

docs.title: The Command Palette
docs.text:
Press Ctrl+K (or Cmd+K on Mac) anywhere in the app.

A modal appears with a search box and list of all available commands grouped by category:

  • Action — fold, score, analyze, explain, design, iterative, MD, cost
  • File — save PDB, save project
  • Import — quick load famous proteins (ubiquitin, insulin, GFP, lysozyme, p53, KRAS, etc.)
  • View — open Dashboard, Academy, toggle theme

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text:
• Type to filter (fuzzy match)
  • ↑/↓ to navigate
  • Enter to execute the highlighted command
  • Esc to close

docs.title: Notifications (Toasts)
docs.text:
Toast notifications appear in the top-right whenever: - A job starts, completes, or fails - An action succeeds (sequence loaded, mutation applied, etc.) - An error occurs

Each toast has: - An icon (✓ success, ✕ error, ⓘ info, ⚠ warning) - A title (sometimes) - A message - A close button (×)

Toasts auto-dismiss after 5 seconds.

docs.title: Analysis Panel
docs.text: The Full Analysis Suite opens a comprehensive analysis overlay with these sections:

docs.title: 1. Sequence Properties
docs.text: QikProp-equivalent table: - Length, MW, isoelectric point (pI) - GRAVY hydropathy - Aromaticity, aliphatic index - Charge at pH 7 - Instability index (with stable/unstable classification) - Extinction coefficient (reduced and oxidized cysteines) - N-terminal half-life (mammalian reticulocytes)

docs.title: 2. Lipinski's Rule of Five
docs.text:
Drug-likeness check: - MW ≤ 500 - H-bond donors ≤ 5 - H-bond acceptors ≤ 10 - LogP ≤ 5

Pass/fail badge with violation list.

docs.title: 3. Hydropathy Profile
docs.text: SVG line chart showing Kyte-Doolittle hydropathy across the sequence with a 9-residue window. Peaks above 1.6 over ~19 residues suggest transmembrane regions.

docs.title: 4. Intrinsic Disorder
docs.text: Per-residue disorder scores with identified disordered regions. p53 famously shows ~70% disorder.

docs.title: 5. Amino Acid Composition
docs.text: Bar chart of all 20 amino acid percentages.

docs.title: 6. Transmembrane Prediction
docs.text: TMHMM-like prediction of membrane-spanning helices.

docs.title: 7. Signal Peptide
docs.text: SignalP-like detection of N-terminal signal peptides for secreted proteins. Shows score and predicted cleavage site.

docs.title: 8. Aggregation Risk
docs.text: TANGO-like prediction of aggregation-prone regions. Color-coded risk level (low/medium/high).

docs.title: 9. Post-Translational Modifications
docs.text:
• Phosphorylation sites by kinase consensus motif (PKA, PKC, CK2, CDK, GSK3, MAPK)
  • N-glycosylation sites (NXS/T sequon)
  • O-glycosylation sites (mucin-like regions)

docs.title: 10. Secondary Structure (if structure is loaded)
docs.text:
• Helix / strand / coil percentages
  • Visual ribbon showing per-residue assignment
  • Radius of gyration
  • SASA estimate

docs.title: 11. Ramachandran Plot
docs.text: Phi/psi scatter for all residues. Background regions show typical alpha-helix (blue) and beta-sheet (orange) zones.

docs.title: 12. Binding Pockets
docs.text: Heuristic pocket detection ranked by score.

docs.title: 13. Bond Network (if structure loaded)
docs.text:
• H-bond count
  • Salt bridge count
  • Disulfide bond count with paired cysteines

docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text:
Click Dashboard in the header (or Cmd+K → Dashboard).

Shows: - Stat cards: structures in session, completed jobs, running jobs, failed jobs, saved projects - Hardware table: CPU, cores, RAM, GPU, backend, tier - Recent jobs table: ID, type, status, progress for the last 10 jobs - Saved projects table: name, structure count, save date

docs.title: Protein Academy
docs.text: Click Academy in the header.

docs.title: Available Levels
docs.text:
• Level 1: Amino Acid Match (5 min, +50 XP)
  • Drag-drop matching game for 8 random amino acids
  • Match the 1-letter code to the full name
  • Level 2: Memory Quiz (3 min, +75 XP)
  • 5 multiple-choice questions about amino acid properties
  • Level 3: Sequence Reader (5 min, +100 XP)
  • Tutorial on reading protein sequences
  • Level 4: Famous Proteins Tour (planned)
  • Level 5: Drug Design 101 (planned)

docs.title: XP and Badges
docs.text: Each completed level awards XP that's displayed in the header badge. Future versions will add badges and a skill tree.

docs.title: Iterative Design
docs.text: The killer feature. Automated protein optimization loop.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
1. Round 0: Fold your starting sequence and score it
  2. Round 1: Use ESM-IF1 to generate N candidate alternatives. Score each.
  3. Keep best: If any candidate scored higher, fold it to confirm and keep it as the new best.
  4. Repeat for the configured number of rounds.

docs.title: Settings
docs.text:
• Rounds (1-10): How many iterations to run
  • Candidates per round (1-20): How many alternatives to try each round

docs.title: Output
docs.text: A panel with: - Initial score → Final score with improvement % - Optimization history chart (score per round) - Best sequence per round with view buttons - Final structure auto-added to your structures list

docs.title: Cost
docs.text:
• 3 rounds × 5 candidates = 15 design samples + ~6 fold operations
  • On CPU: ~30 minutes for a 50-residue protein
  • On GPU: ~2 minutes

docs.title: Component Manager
docs.text: The Component Manager (header → Components) is the primary way to install, update, and uninstall the 11 ML engines bundled with OpenDNA. It behaves like a package catalog — nothing heavy is installed until you ask for it.

docs.title: The 11 engines
docs.text:
Engine Category Size Purpose ESMFold Folding ~2.0 GB Single-chain structure prediction ColabFold Folding ~3.5 GB MSA-based folding (AlphaFold2 weights) Boltz-1 Multimer ~4.2 GB Complex / multimer structure prediction ESM-IF1 Design ~0.6 GB Inverse folding / sequence design RFdiffusion Design ~1.8 GB Backbone generation from scratch DiffDock Docking ~1.1 GB Diffusion-based ligand docking AutoDock Vina Docking ~0.05 GB Classic lattice-based docking OpenMM Dynamics ~0.3 GB Molecular dynamics with AMBER14 + TIP3P xTB QM ~0.2 GB Semi-empirical quantum mechanics ANI-2x NNP ~0.5 GB Neural-network potential for small molecules Ollama (llama3.2:3b) LLM ~2.0 GB On-device chat and protein explanations

Totals are displayed at the top of the manager and pulled live from disk via GET /v1/components/disk-usage.

docs.title: Install / Uninstall
docs.text:
Every card exposes four states: Not installed, Installing (NN%), Installed, and Update available. Clicking Install calls POST /v1/components/install which streams progress through a Server-Sent Events channel. Installs are resumable — if you close the app mid-download it will pick up where it left off the next time.

Uninstall removes the weights directory and the HuggingFace cache entry. OpenDNA never touches other caches outside ~/.opendna/components/.

docs.title: Categories
docs.text: The sidebar groups engines by Folding, Design, Docking, Dynamics, QM / NNP, Multimer, and LLM. Filter chips at the top let you narrow to one category. A search box performs case-insensitive substring matching on name, description, and citation.

docs.title: Disk usage
docs.text: At the bottom of the manager you'll see a sparkline-style bar for each installed engine, plus a total across all components. Click Clean up to remove any orphaned files (e.g. partial downloads from crashed installs).

docs.title: Visual Workflow Editor
docs.text: Press Workflow in the header to open the node-based pipeline editor powered by React Flow. This replaces hand-written Python scripts for most end-to-end pipelines.

docs.title: The canvas
docs.text: A zoomable, pannable infinite canvas. Drag from the node palette on the left onto the canvas to add a node. Connect nodes by dragging from an output port (right side) to an input port (left side). Edges are typed — an output of type Structure will only connect to inputs that accept Structure.

docs.title: The 10 node types
docs.text: Node Inputs Outputs Description Sequence — seq: str Literal amino acid sequence entered by the user Import UniProt accession seq, metadata Fetches from UniProt REST Import PDB pdb_id structure Fetches from RCSB Fold seq structure, plddt Runs ESMFold / ColabFold / Boltz Design structure candidates[] Runs ESM-IF1 or RFdiffusion Score seq or structure score, breakdown Sequence + structure scoring Analyze seq, structure analysis (full 18+ suite) Runs the full analysis suite Dock structure, ligand poses[], affinity DiffDock or AutoDock Vina MD structure trajectory, rmsd, rmsf OpenMM molecular dynamics Export anything — Writes to file (PDB / PNG / SVG / GLTF / OBJ / YAML)

docs.title: 5 built-in templates
docs.text:
Click Templates to spawn a pre-built graph:

  1. Fold → Score — Classic 2-node pipeline
  2. Fold → Design → Evaluate — Iterative design loop
  3. Fold → Dock → MD — Structure-based drug design chain
  4. Import PDB → Analyze — Analysis-only pipeline for an existing structure
  5. Import UniProt → Fold → Compare AF DB — Benchmark against AlphaFold DB

docs.title: Save / Load
docs.text:
Click Save to serialize the current canvas to YAML:

nodes:
  - id: n1
    type: sequence
    data:
      value: "MKTVRQERLK"
  - id: n2
    type: fold
    data:
      engine: esmfold
edges:
  - source: n1
    target: n2
    sourceHandle: seq
    targetHandle: seq

YAML files live in ~/.opendna/workflows/. Drag a .yaml file onto the canvas to load it.

docs.title: Provenance
docs.text: Every run of a workflow automatically writes a provenance DAG. Each node execution produces a record_step call with inputs, outputs, timestamps, engine version, and a content hash. You can later use diff_steps, blame_residue, and bisect_regression to investigate differences between runs.

docs.title: Real-Time Collaboration
docs.text: The Collab button in the header opens the real-time collaboration panel. It uses Yjs CRDTs over a y-websocket-compatible relay so multiple users can edit the same project simultaneously without conflicts.

docs.title: Rooms
docs.text: A room is identified by a UUID and is pinned to a specific project. Click New room to get a URL you can paste to a collaborator. Anyone with the URL can join.

docs.title: Cursors & presence
docs.text: You see every teammate's cursor in the viewer (labeled with their username / color). The Collab panel lists who's currently in the room and how long they've been idle.

docs.title: Notes tab
docs.text: A shared rich-text document powered by y-prosemirror. Use it for lab notebook entries that everyone in the room can edit together. Changes sync in under 200 ms on a LAN. Formatting: bold, italic, headings, bullet lists, code blocks, $latex$ math.

docs.title: Residue comments tab
docs.text: Click any residue in the 3D viewer, then click Comment. The comment is pinned to that residue and visible to everyone in the room, forever. Useful for review meetings ("I think R42 is the catalytic nucleophile — thoughts?"). Resolved comments are archived but preserved for provenance.

docs.title: Offline tolerance
docs.text: If you lose your network connection the Yjs document keeps accepting edits locally. When you reconnect, the CRDT merge is automatic and conflict-free.

docs.title: Protein Search
docs.text: The Search button in the header opens a unified search overlay covering UniProt, RCSB PDB, and AlphaFold DB.

docs.title: UniProt full-text search
docs.text:
Under the hood this calls GET /v1/search/uniprot which proxies to the UniProt REST API. You can search by:

  • Free text — any keyword matching name, function, or organism
  • Reviewed only — restrict to Swiss-Prot manually reviewed entries
  • Organism — scientific or common name (e.g. Homo sapiens, E. coli)
  • Length range — min/max residues
  • Has structure — only entries with an AlphaFold DB or PDB entry

Results show accession, protein name, organism, length, and a review badge. Click any row to load that protein into the Tools tab.

docs.title: PDB search
docs.text: Full-text search over RCSB PDB titles and abstracts, plus experimental method filter (X-ray, NMR, cryo-EM).

docs.title: AlphaFold DB fetch
docs.text: Enter a UniProt accession to pull the pre-computed AlphaFold DB structure without running a fold.

docs.title: Academy: Levels 4–7 and Mini-games
docs.text: The Protein Academy gained four new levels and three standalone mini-games in v0.5.0.

docs.title: New levels
docs.text:
• Level 4: Secondary Structure (+100 XP) — Interactive helix / sheet / coil labelling exercise
  • Level 5: Binding Sites (+125 XP) — Identify catalytic residues in chymotrypsin, lysozyme, HIV protease
  • Level 6: Drug Design 101 (+150 XP) — Walkthrough of fragment-based drug discovery
  • Level 7: Fold Recognition (+200 XP) — Classify folds into SCOP superfamilies

docs.title: 13 badges
docs.text:
Earn badges by completing challenges:

Rookie, Folder, Designer, Dynamicist, Crystallographer, Inverse Guru, Compiler, Night Owl, Speed Demon, Mutagenist, Cavity Seeker, Multimerist, Streak Master (7-day streak).

docs.title: 7 daily-challenge templates
docs.text: Each day a challenge is rotated from one of 7 templates (mutate-and-fold, spot-the-difference, predict-pLDDT, etc.). Completing adds to your streak.

docs.title: 23-term glossary
docs.text: A searchable glossary of 23 fundamental terms (alpha helix, beta sheet, pLDDT, RMSD, etc.). Each term has an illustrated flashcard that links to the Molstar viewer for examples.

docs.title: SQLite leaderboard + streaks
docs.text: Your XP, badges, and streak are persisted to ~/.opendna/academy.db. The leaderboard view shows top 100 locally (or globally if you opt in to the optional cloud sync).

docs.title: Mini-games
docs.text:
Standalone games accessible from the Mini-games header button without having to enter the Academy:

  1. AA Match — Fast-paced drag-drop matching game. Score rises with speed and accuracy.
  2. Build-a-Helix — Place residues onto a helical wheel to maximize amphipathicity.
  3. Famous Proteins Tour — Guided exploration of ubiquitin, insulin, GFP, lysozyme, myoglobin, p53, KRAS, and EGFR with one-paragraph stories.

docs.title: Provenance & Time Machine
docs.text: Every non-trivial action in OpenDNA is recorded as a provenance step — a node in a DAG that captures inputs, outputs, timestamps, engine versions, and content hashes. This powers the time-machine feature and makes reproducibility automatic.

docs.title: record_step
docs.text:
The primitive:

from opendna.provenance import record_step

step_id = record_step(
    operation="fold",
    inputs={"sequence": "MKTV..."},
    outputs={"pdb_hash": "sha256:..."},
    engine="esmfold",
    engine_version="1.0.3",
    parent_ids=["step_abc123"],
)

Most steps are recorded automatically — you rarely call this directly.

docs.title: diff_steps
docs.text:
Compare two steps (or two runs) and get a structured diff:

from opendna.provenance import diff_steps
d = diff_steps("step_a", "step_b")
print(d.rmsd, d.plddt_delta, d.mutation_list)

docs.title: blame_residue
docs.text:
Given a structure and a residue, walk the DAG backwards to find the exact step that introduced the current value:

from opendna.provenance import blame_residue
source = blame_residue(structure_id="s_42", residue_index=48)
print(source.operation, source.step_id)

docs.title: bisect_regression
docs.text:
Binary-search across the history of a project to find when a regression was introduced:

from opendna.provenance import bisect_regression
culprit = bisect_regression(
    project_id="proj_1",
    good_step="step_initial",
    bad_step="step_latest",
    test=lambda s: s.mean_plddt >= 80,
)

docs.title: Time machine UI
docs.text: Click History in the bottom bar to open a timeline scrubber. Drag the handle to rewind the project to any earlier state — your structures, analyses, and notes revert atomically. Release and you're back at the latest state.

docs.title: Priority Job Queue & WebSocket Streaming
docs.text: v0.5.0 replaces the old flat job list with a 3-tier priority queue plus real-time WebSocket streaming.

docs.title: The 3 tiers
docs.text:
Tier Use case Pre-emption interactive Small jobs from the UI Never pre-empted normal Standard jobs Pre-empted by interactive if GPU is saturated batch Overnight / bulk runs Pre-empted by both other tiers

Enqueue with:

curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8765/v1/queue/enqueue \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"type": "fold", "priority": "interactive", "payload": {"sequence": "MKTV..."}}'

docs.title: GPU pool + warm-model cache
docs.text: The scheduler keeps the most recently used model in GPU memory so back-to-back jobs against the same engine pay zero load time. LRU eviction when memory is tight.

docs.title: WebSocket streaming
docs.text:
Subscribe to a job's events:

const ws = new WebSocket(`ws://127.0.0.1:8765/v1/ws/jobs/${jobId}`);
ws.onmessage = (ev) => {
  const msg = JSON.parse(ev.data);
  console.log(msg.phase, msg.progress, msg.partial_result);
};

Events include queued, started, progress, partial_result, completed, failed.

docs.title: Lab Notebook & Zenodo DOI Minting
docs.text: The header's Notebook entry (inside Save → Notebook) opens a per-project lab notebook.

docs.title: Notebook features
docs.text:
• Rich text with $latex$ math and code blocks
  • Embed any structure, figure, or analysis from the current project inline
  • Auto-populated timestamps from provenance steps
  • Export to Markdown, PDF, or .ipynb

docs.title: Mint a DOI on Zenodo
docs.text:
When you're ready to publish:

  1. Go to Notebook → Publish → Zenodo
  2. Enter your Zenodo token (or set ZENODO_TOKEN in your environment)
  3. Fill in title, authors, keywords, description
  4. Click Mint DOI

OpenDNA bundles the entire project (provenance DAG, structures, notebook, SBOM, YAML workflow) into a single .zip, uploads it, and returns the permanent DOI. The DOI is pinned into the notebook for future reference.

docs.title: Figure & 3D Export
docs.text:
Click Save → Export to open the export dialog:

  • PNG / SVG figure — Snapshot of the current viewer at any resolution (up to 8K). SVG is vector.
  • GLTF / OBJ 3D mesh — Export the current representation as a 3D mesh for use in Blender, Maya, or a web WebGL scene.
  • Animated GIF trajectory — Record a short GIF of the protein rotating, or of an MD trajectory played back.
  • Multi-frame PDB — Write every frame of an MD trajectory as a single PDB with MODEL records.

All exports write to your chosen path and log a provenance step.

docs.title: Ramachandran Plot
docs.text:
Click Ramachandran in the header (or press R) to open the interactive phi/psi scatter plot.

  • Each dot is a residue. Background density contours show favored, allowed, and disallowed regions.
  • Hover a dot to see residue number, amino acid, phi, psi.
  • Click a dot — the corresponding residue is highlighted in the 3D viewer and scrolled into view.
  • Filter by region: click Favored, Allowed, or Outlier chips to show only residues in that region.
  • Export to PNG or SVG with one click.

This is the fastest way to spot structural outliers in a predicted model.

docs.title: PQC Auth Layer
docs.text: When OPENDNA_AUTH_REQUIRED=1 is set, every API call must carry Authorization: Bearer <api-key> and every user is authenticated with post-quantum crypto.

docs.title: Key primitives
docs.text:
• ML-KEM-768 — NIST-standardized post-quantum KEM, used for wrapping the user's API key at login
  • ML-DSA-65 — NIST-standardized post-quantum signature, used to sign every audit log entry

These are provided by liboqs-python. OpenDNA does not roll its own crypto.

docs.title: Endpoints
docs.text:
• POST /v1/auth/register — create a user (first user becomes admin)
  • POST /v1/auth/login — authenticate and receive a wrapped API key
  • POST /v1/auth/rotate — rotate your API key
  • GET /v1/auth/me — your profile
  • POST /v1/auth/api-keys — mint a new API key with custom scopes

docs.title: Audit log
docs.text:
Every request is appended to ~/.opendna/audit.log with a hash-chained, ML-DSA-65-signed entry. The log is verified on server startup; if tampering is detected the server refuses to boot. Inspect the log with:

opendna audit verify
opendna audit tail --n 100

docs.title: GDPR export & erasure
docs.text:
A user can request a full export of their data or ask for erasure:

curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8765/v1/compliance/gdpr/export \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" \
  -d '{"user_id": "alice"}' -o alice.zip

curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8765/v1/compliance/gdpr/erase \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" \
  -d '{"user_id": "alice"}'

Erasure is permanent and audit-logged but leaves hashes in the audit log so the chain remains verifiable.

docs.title: React error boundaries
docs.text: Every major panel is wrapped in an ErrorBoundary. When a panel crashes you see a friendly card with the error message, a "Report" button (which writes to ~/.opendna/crashes/ with secrets redacted), and a "Retry" button — instead of the whole UI going blank.

docs.title: @retry decorator
docs.text: Python-side engine calls are wrapped in a @retry(attempts=3, backoff=exp) decorator so transient errors (HuggingFace 5xx, connection resets) don't fail the whole job.

docs.title: SelfHealer background thread
docs.text:
A daemon thread checks every 30 s for common failure modes and repairs them:

  • Stale lock files in ~/.opendna/jobs/
  • Orphaned GPU allocations
  • Corrupt SQLite journal files
  • Dangling PyInstaller temp directories

All repairs are logged and auditable.

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text:
Shortcut Action Ctrl+K / Cmd+K Open command palette Ctrl+/ Open command palette F Fold current sequence S Score current sequence A Run analysis suite Esc Close any open overlay Ctrl+S Save current PDB Ctrl+Shift+S Save project workspace

Shortcuts that don't conflict with text inputs work everywhere. Single-letter shortcuts only work when not focused on an input.

docs.title: Speed up CPU folding
docs.text:
• Use shorter sequences first to test (under 50 aa folds in seconds)
  • The result cache means folding the same sequence twice is instant
  • If you have NVIDIA GPU, install CUDA torch for ~50x speedup

docs.title: Get richer AI explanations
docs.text:
Install Ollama and pull a small model: 

ollama pull llama3.2:3b
ollama serve  # in background
Then "Explain This Protein" uses real LLM responses instead of the heuristic fallback.

docs.title: Analyze multiple proteins
docs.text: Use the Structures tab to keep a history. The compare button lets you put two side-by-side.

docs.title: Find binding pockets
docs.text: Run Full Analysis Suite on a folded protein. The "Putative Binding Pockets" section ranks the top 5 cavities.

docs.title: Understand your protein in plain English
docs.text: Use Explain This Protein (AI) for a friendly summary that even a high school student can understand.
